name: التَّكْوِير
summary: Soura At-Takwir is a powerful Meccan revelation that vividly describes the cataclysmic events of the Day of Judgment. The soura opens with striking imagery of cosmic upheaval: the sun being wrapped in darkness, stars falling from their courses, mountains crumbling, seas boiling, and the sky being stripped away. These apocalyptic descriptions serve to convey the absolute certainty and magnitude of the final hour, warning humanity that the current order of the universe will be completely transformed. The soura then transitions to address the authenticity of the Qur'an, refuting the accusations of the disbelievers who claimed Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) was mad or that the revelation came from jinn. The Qur'an is affirmed as a word conveyed by the noble angel Jibril (Gabriel), who holds a high position with the Owner of the Throne—obeyed in the heavens and trustworthy. The Prophet is defended as neither a madman nor a withholder of knowledge of the unseen. Key spiritual lessons include the inevitability of accountability—every soul will know what it has brought forward—and the importance of recognizing the Qur'an as divine guidance for all humanity. The soura concludes by emphasizing that human will is ultimately subject to Allah's supreme will, reinforcing the Islamic concept of divine sovereignty and the universal nature of the prophetic message.

name: الإِنْفِطَار
summary: Soura Al-Infitar is a concise yet profound Meccan revelation that addresses the reality of the Day of Judgment and the nature of human accountability before Allah. The soura opens with dramatic descriptions of the sky breaking apart, stars scattering, seas erupting, and graves being overturned—signifying the complete dissolution of the worldly order as we know it. The central question posed is: 'What has deceived you concerning your Lord, the Generous?' This rhetorical challenge addresses the human tendency toward heedlessness despite Allah's countless blessings of creation and sustenance. The soura emphasizes that Allah has created, proportioned, and balanced human beings in perfect form, yet people persist in denying the Recompense. An important theological concept introduced is the presence of recording angels (Kiraman Katibin) who document every action, underscoring that nothing escapes divine awareness or observation. The soura concludes with the stark contrast between the fate of the righteous, who will be in pleasure, and the wicked, who will burn in Hellfire. The final verses emphasize the absolute sovereignty of Allah on that Day, when no soul can avail another and all command belongs entirely to Allah. This soura serves as a wake-up call to recognize Allah's benevolence and prepare for the inevitable meeting with the Creator.

name: المُطَفِّفِين
summary: Soura Al-Mutaffifin is a Meccan revelation that condemns fraudulent business practices and upholds the principles of justice and integrity. The soura opens with a severe warning against those who give less than what is due when measuring for others, yet demand full measure when receiving. This practice of 'at-taftif' (defrauding) was prevalent in pre-Islamic Arabian markets and remains a universal human failing. The soura connects this economic dishonesty to a deeper spiritual malady: the denial of the Day of Recompense. Those who cheat in worldly transactions often do so because they have lost sight of accountability before Allah. The soura introduces the concept of two registers: 'Sijjin' for the record of the wicked and 'Illiyyun' for the record of the righteous, symbolizing the profound separation between the two categories of humanity. The righteous are described as being in pleasure on adorned couches, their faces radiant with joy, while the wicked face torment. The soura concludes with a poignant reversal: the disbelievers who once mocked the believers will themselves be mocked on the Day of Judgment. This soura teaches that economic justice is not merely a social contract but a divine imperative, and that those who prioritize honest dealing are preparing their eternal success.

name: الإِنْشِقَاق
summary: Soura Al-Inshiqaq is a Meccan revelation that portrays the dramatic events of the Day of Judgment and the sorting of humanity into two distinct groups. The soura opens with the image of the sky splitting open and the earth extending and casting out its contents, both responding to their Lord's command—a powerful metaphor for the totality of creation's submission to divine authority. The central message addresses all of humanity: 'You are laboring toward your Lord with great exertion and will meet it.' This verse serves as both a warning and encouragement, reminding people that their entire lives are a journey toward divine encounter. The soura then distinguishes between those who receive their records in their right hands (indicating salvation and ease) and those who receive theirs behind their backs (indicating damnation and regret). The latter group cries out for destruction, having lived in false security, thinking they would never return to Allah. The soura concludes by swearing by natural phenomena—the twilight glow, the night, the full moon—to affirm that human beings will experience successive states, questioning why they persist in disbelief and failure to prostrate when the Qur'an is recited. The final verse offers hope: for those who believe and do righteous deeds, there is a reward uninterrupted.

name: البُرُوج
summary: Soura Al-Buruj is a Meccan revelation that addresses the persecution of believers and affirms divine justice and the protection of the Qur'an. The soura opens with oaths by the sky adorned with great stars, the promised Day, and the witness and what is witnessed—establishing the certainty of the Hereafter. The central historical reference is to the 'companions of the trench' (Ashab al-Ukhdud), who dug a massive trench and burned believers alive, persecuting them solely for their faith in Allah, the Exalted in Might, the Praiseworthy. This likely refers to historical persecution events, possibly the Najran persecution in Yemen or similar incidents. The soura emphasizes that the tormentors had no justification beyond hatred of true faith. Divine retribution is assured: those who torture believing men and women without repentance will face Hellfire, while those who believe and do righteous deeds will receive gardens beneath which rivers flow. The soura then references the armies of Pharaoh and Thamud as examples of destroyed tyrants, warning that disbelievers persist in denial despite Allah encompassing them from behind. The soura concludes by affirming the honor and preservation of the Qur'an, inscribed in a Preserved Slate (al-Lawh al-Mahfuz), ensuring its protection from corruption. This soura teaches that faith may invite persecution, but ultimate victory belongs to the believers.

name: الطَّارِق
summary: Soura At-Tariq is a concise Meccan revelation that uses celestial imagery to affirm divine oversight, human creation, and the power of resurrection. The soura opens with oaths by the sky and the night comer (at-tariq), identified as the piercing star that appears in the darkness—a metaphor for divine guidance that illuminates the darkness of ignorance. The soura then introduces the concept of divine guardianship: every soul has a protector (qarín) watching over it, whether an angel or a recording angel, emphasizing that human beings are never outside of Allah's awareness. The central reflection invites humanity to contemplate their humble origins—created from a fluid (semen) emerging between the backbone and ribs—to recognize that the same God who brought them into existence from such a state is fully capable of resurrecting them. The Day of Judgment is described as the moment when secrets will be laid bare and no human being will have power or any helper. The soura concludes by swearing by the sky that returns rain and the earth that splits open, affirming that the Qur'an is a 'decisive statement' (kalimun qat'i)—not mere amusement. The final verses address the disbelievers' plotting against the Prophet and the divine counter-plan, instructing patience: 'Allow time for the disbelievers. Leave them awhile.'

name: الأَعْلَى
summary: Soura Al-A'la is a Meccan revelation that glorifies Allah's supreme nature, affirms the preservation of the Qur'an, and distinguishes between the righteous and the heedless. The soura opens with the command to exalt the name of Allah, the Most High (al-A'la), who created, proportioned, destined, and guided creation—establishing a comprehensive theology of divine creative and providential power. The natural cycle of vegetation—bringing forth pasture and then reducing it to withered stubble—serves as a metaphor for the stages of human life and the transience of worldly existence. A significant promise is made to Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him): 'We will make you recite, and you will not forget, except what Allah should will.' This verse is understood as a guarantee of the Qur'an's preservation through the Prophet's recitation. The soura then contrasts two categories of people: the God-fearing (muttaqin) who benefit from the reminder, and the wretched (ashqa) who avoid it and will burn in the greatest Fire, experiencing neither death nor life. Success is defined as self-purification, mentioning Allah's name, and establishing prayer. The soura criticizes preference for worldly life over the Hereafter, noting that the Hereafter is better and more enduring. The soura concludes by affirming continuity with previous scriptures—those of Abraham and Moses—positioning the Qur'an as the culmination of a single divine message.

name: الغَاشِيَة
summary: Soura Al-Ghashiyah is a Meccan revelation that presents a stark contrast between the fates of the blessed and the damned on the Day of Judgment, followed by an invitation to reflect on Allah's creative signs. The soura opens by asking if news of the Overwhelming event has reached the listener—a rhetorical device emphasizing the magnitude and inescapability of the Hereafter. The damned are described with vivid sensory details: faces humbled, working and exhausted, burning in an intensely hot Fire, given drink from a boiling spring, and fed only poisonous, thorny plants that neither nourish nor relieve hunger. In contrast, the blessed will have radiant, pleased faces, enjoying elevated gardens with flowing springs, raised couches, cups, cushions, and carpets—imagery conveying complete comfort and delight. The soura then shifts to a powerful invitation to reflection (tadabbur): examining the creation of camels, the raising of the sky, the erecting of mountains, and the spreading of the earth. These natural phenomena serve as signs (ayat) of Allah's omnipotence and wisdom. The soura concludes by instructing the Prophet (peace be upon him) to remind, emphasizing his role as a warner, not a controller of hearts. Those who turn away face the greatest punishment, and all will return to Allah for their account. This soura teaches that recognition of divine power in creation should lead to gratitude and heedfulness.

name: الفَجْر
summary: Soura Al-Fajr is a Meccan revelation that combines historical admonition with moral critique and culminates in one of the most beautiful addresses in the Qur'an. The soura opens with oaths by the dawn, the ten nights (interpreted as the last ten nights of Ramadan or the first ten of Dhul Hijjah), the even and the odd, and the passing night—establishing divine authority through natural phenomena. The historical section recounts the destruction of powerful tyrannical nations: 'Aad with their lofty pillars of Iram, Thamud who carved rocks in the valleys, and Pharaoh 'owner of the stakes'—all of whom oppressed people and spread corruption, only to face divine scourge. The soura then offers a penetrating psychological analysis of human ingratitude: when blessed, people credit themselves ('My Lord has honored me'), but when tested, they blame Allah ('My Lord has humiliated me'). This is followed by a moral indictment: failure to honor orphans, feed the poor, consume inheritance justly, and excessive love of wealth. The Day of Judgment is depicted with earth leveled and crushed, Allah arriving with angels rank upon rank, and Hell brought into view. The sinner's regret is heartbreaking: 'Oh, I wish I had sent ahead some good for my life.' The soura concludes with one of the Qur'an's most beloved addresses: 'O reassured soul (nafs al-mutma'innah), return to your Lord, well-pleased and pleasing to Him, and enter among My righteous servants, and enter My Paradise.'

name: البَلَد
summary: Soura Al-Balad is a Meccan revelation that reflects on human struggle, divine oversight, and the moral responsibility that comes with free will. The soura opens with an oath by 'this city' (Makkah), where the Prophet (peace be upon him) is described as being free of restriction—a reference to the sacred and protected status of the Prophet in the holy city. The 'father and that which was born of him' is interpreted as referring to Adam and all his descendants, establishing the universality of the message. The soura declares that Allah has created humanity 'into hardship' (fi kasb)—a statement about the fundamental nature of human existence as one of struggle, effort, and responsibility. The central moral question addresses human arrogance: does anyone think they will never be overcome? Does wealth or self-sufficiency make one immune to divine accountability? The soura affirms that Allah has given humanity eyes, a tongue, and lips—the faculties of perception, speech, and communication—and has shown the 'two ways' (al-tariqayn): the path of faith and good, and the path of disbelief and evil. However, most people fail to 'break through the difficult pass' (al-'aqaq). This difficult pass is defined through specific acts of compassion: freeing a slave, feeding the hungry (especially orphans and the destitute), and being among those who believe and counsel each other to patience and compassion. The soura concludes by distinguishing between the companions of the right (the righteous) and the companions of the left (the disbelievers), whose fire will close in upon them—a powerful image of the ultimate consequences of one's choices.
